prompt:
Our test job went green suspiciously fast after moving to the current JUnit Platform entry point: the report contains the four Jupiter invocations but none of the legacy inventory cases. Running the old class directly used to execute its setup/cleanup and treat its declared expected exception as a pass. The protected local notes and platform contract define what the mixed migration must retain.

Please repair the repository's platform plan and legacy engine boundary so discovery is complete without splitting CI back into separate runners. Jupiter's three parameter values and per-invocation lifecycle must stay intact. Each legacy case needs fresh lifecycle, cleanup even when the body throws, and correct expected-exception semantics; an expected exception that never occurs is still a failure. Preserve original unexpected failure types rather than converting every throw into success.

Run `java TestMain.java`. TestMain, all fixture test classes, the probe, the platform contract, and migration notes are protected.

full_distill.primary_skill: migrate a mixed Java test estate to one current platform plan while preserving engine discovery, lifecycle, parameter expansion, and legacy expected-failure semantics
full_distill.baseline_evidence: java TestMain.java exits 1 because the platform plan reports only four current invocations, silently omits both legacy cases, and the stale vintage adapter marks a no-throw expected-exception counterexample as passed without lifecycle hooks
full_distill.success_contract: one platform execution reports four current and two legacy passing cases, runs before and after around every invocation, expands all parameter values, and fails a declared expected exception when the body returns normally
adversarial_factors: the protected result IDs require both selected classes and all three parameter values, rejecting a green build obtained by narrowing selectors or dropping an engine
an exact lifecycle event stream surrounds current parameters and legacy normal and throwing bodies, so bulk setup, skipped cleanup, and one shared legacy instance do not satisfy the contract
a protected counterexample declares an expected exception but returns normally and must fail with cleanup evidence, rejecting adapters that blindly bless expected annotations
the real legacy expected case throws an assignable exception and must pass with a specific detail, while unexpected exception types remain observable rather than broadly swallowed
